Row1.Rationale: Cemiplimab is a PD‑1–blocking antibody; by interrupting PD‑1/PD‑L1 signaling, it releases inhibitory brakes on tumor‑reactive T cells, enabling cytotoxic activity against NSCLC cells that express PD‑L1 and present cognate antigens.

Row2.Rationale: Reactivated effector T cells increase IFN‑γ production, which drives JAK‑STAT–dependent transcription of immune‑stimulatory genes in tumor and stromal cells, reinforcing antigen presentation and Th1‑polarized immunity that support PD‑1 blockade efficacy.

Row3.Rationale: Productive recognition of tumor antigens by T cells in the context of MHC is a prerequisite for benefit from PD‑1 inhibition; intact antigen‑recognition pathways allow cemiplimab‑reinvigorated T cells to selectively target NSCLC cells.

Row4.Rationale: Workshop sessions on cancer immunology emphasize that enhancing T‑cell activation and co‑stimulatory signaling improves antitumor immunity; PD‑1 blockade synergizes with these pathways to expand and sustain cytotoxic T‑cell responses in solid tumors, including lung cancer.

Row5.Rationale: Immune‑regulation sessions highlight networks that restrain effector T cells; by blocking PD‑1, cemiplimab diminishes one major inhibitory circuit, shifting the balance toward effector over regulatory programs in the tumor microenvironment.

Row6.Rationale: Abstracts describe a PI3Kδ–FOXO–FasL amplification loop that rewires T‑helper signaling and effector programming; PD‑1 blockade can favor activation over exhaustion, allowing such intrinsic T‑cell pathways to promote cytotoxicity against tumor targets.

Row7.Pathway ID/Name: NK and cytotoxic T‑cell survival pathways
Row7.Rationale: Cancer‑immunology presentations report that survival of NK and cytotoxic T cells is essential for durable antitumor responses; PD‑1 inhibition supports persistence and function of these cytotoxic populations, contributing to cemiplimab activity in NSCLC.

Row8.Rationale: Resident memory T cells in solid tumors mediate potent local immunity; workshop data on tumor‑specific TRM responses imply that relieving PD‑1‑mediated inhibition can unleash their antitumor activity, improving responses to PD‑1 blockade in lung tumors.

Row9.Rationale: Sessions on engineering T cells (e.g., CAR‑T with tuned TCR‑controlled “fuzzy logic”) show how optimized signaling networks can be combined with checkpoint inhibition; cemiplimab can further enhance the function of engineered T cells infiltrating NSCLC.

Row10.Rationale: Imaging and myeloid‑landscape studies in solid tumors demonstrate that suppressive myeloid cells can inhibit T‑cell function; effective PD‑1 blockade partially overcomes this by strengthening T‑cell effector programs, improving the T‑cell/myeloid balance in the tumor microenvironment.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: B‑cell and antibody‑mediated modulation of antitumor immunity
Row11.Rationale: Workshop content on B‑cell responses and antibody production indicates that B cells can contribute to antigen presentation and tertiary lymphoid structure formation; PD‑1 inhibition may augment these processes, indirectly supporting T‑cell–mediated control of NSCLC.

Row12.Rationale: Systems‑immunology sessions emphasize that effective immunotherapy reflects coordinated changes across T, B, myeloid, and stromal compartments; cemiplimab’s PD‑1 blockade acts as a nodal perturbation that re‑wires these networks toward an inflamed, tumor‑rejecting state.
